A seventh grader broke his wrist in gym class. Nobody did anything wrong. The school still got sued. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)

Your general liability policy only pays if your school was negligent. Most student injuries involve no negligence at all. So the bill lands on the family, and that is where the lawsuit starts. Here is the gap nobody walked you through:

Most student injuries involve no negligence, which means your liability policy will not pay them. Here is the coverage that does, and how to confirm you actually have it. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)

Post_Text: A kid gets hurt at your school doing something completely ordinary. Your liability carrier looks at the claim and says, correctly, you owe nothing. The family still has a few thousand dollars in bills. Guess who they call next:

Student accident insurance pays a hurt kid's medical bills no matter whose fault it was. A family whose bills got handled rarely goes looking for a lawyer. The coverage that pays on the front end is the one that prevents the lawsuit on the back end:

There is one number on your athletics plan that decides everything. The catastrophic injury limit. A serious spinal or head injury can run into the hundreds of thousands over a lifetime of care. Check that number before the season starts, not after:

'Primary' or 'excess' is the most important word on your student accident plan. An excess plan pays nothing for a family that has no health insurance to go first. If you serve uninsured families and bought excess, you bought a gap:

One question for whoever handles your school's insurance: when a student gets hurt this year and nobody was at fault, is there a plan that pays toward their bills? Does it pay first, or only after the family's own insurance? If the answer is a shrug, read this:
